data.title: Top global stories this week: Rolls-Royce, Alibaba and Ferrari among notable names
data.description:
February lived up to its reputation of being a traditionally weak month for markets, as most major stock indexes ended mixed on a weekly basis.

Among key factors swaying stocks in February was the Trump administration’s flurry of tariff actions against trading partners Mexico, China and Canada.

Sentiment also took a hit from a post-earnings fall in semiconductor giant Nvidia (NVDA). The Silicon Valley company provided a quarterly gross margin forecast weaker than expected, which overshadowed fourth-quarter results beat.

Meanwhile, recent data highlighted persistent inflation and an economic slowdown, raising concerns. These signals prompted the Federal Reserve to signal its readiness to keep interest rates steady.

S&P 500 index (SP500) notched up a record high on Friday but ended the week 0.5% lower and Nasdaq 100 (COMP:IND) fell 2.3%. On the contrary, Dow Jones (DJI) advanced 0.9% in the week.

Across the Atlantic, the European equities (STOXX) notched a fresh record high of 560 on Wednesday, and ended the week 0.7% higher. Investors assessed a slew of mixed corporate earnings from industry giants, while US President Donald Trump 's announcement of a forthcoming 25% tariff on imports from the European Union, weighed on economic sentiment.

London (UKX) equities advanced 1.7% in the week, with France (CAC:IND) and Germany (DAX:IND) gaining 0.3%, and 0.6%, respectively.

Major news from Europe included leadership changes, strong earnings in automotive and beverages, acquisitions in tech and food delivery, and share buybacks in automotive and aerospace.

Ferrari’s (RACE) major shareholder Exor said it plans to sell 7M shares in the Italian supercar company, of which Ferrari will buy back 10%, or up to €300M.

European food delivery giant Just Eat Takeaway.com (OTCPK:JTKWY) is poised to be acquired by Dutch technology investor Prosus (OTCPK:PROSY) in an all-cash deal worth roughly 4.1B euros ($4.3B).

Rolls-Royce (OTCPK:RYCEY) shares rose to an all-time high on Thursday after the company resumed shareholder payouts, launched a £1B (~$1.27B) share buyback, and raised medium-term guidance.

Unilever (UL) announced that Hein Schumacher will resign as chief executive and board director on March 1, 2025, with current finance chief Fernando Fernandez set to succeed him as CEO.

Beer giant Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD) overcame weak results in China to top earnings expectations.

Stellantis (STLA) provided a cautious outlook for 2025 after its annual results were impacted by a decline in its U.S. business last year, which resulted in the dismissal of Carlos Tavares as CEO.

The Asia-Pacific markets fell over the week, reflecting a global selloff driven by declines in technology stocks following Nvidia’s results. Growing concerns about a potential trade war also weighed as Trump announced an additional 10% duty on Chinese imports.

China (SHCOMP) fell 1.4% and Hong Kong (HSI) lost 1.1%. People’s Bank of China intensified efforts to stabilize the yuan, injecting CNY 300B through a one-year medium-term lending facility while keeping the rate at 2% to support liquidity.

Japan's retail sales grew in January 2025, marking the 34th consecutive month of expansion and the fastest growth since February 2024, though slightly below market expectations. In the week, Japan markets (NKY:IND) dropped 2.8%.

Asian corporate news revolved around AI updates and increase in product lines.

Chinese tech company Alibaba (BABA) unveiled plans to invest at least 380B yuan ($52.44B) in its cloud computing and artificial intelligence infrastructure over the next three years.

DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) is pushing for an early launch of its new large language model, following the Chinese AI startup's global hit the R1 model released in January.

Chinese technology company Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF) launched its latest flagship smartphone, the Xiaomi 15 Ultra, priced at 6,499 yuan ($894.19).

Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) launched a new artificial intelligence model, called the Hunyuan Turbo S, which it claims is faster than DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) R1, Hunyuan T1, and other slow thinking models.

Chinese internet search giant Baidu (BIDU) plans to launch the next generation of its AI model, Ernie 4.5, with improved capabilities on March 16.

Japanese airline ANA plans to order up to 77 aircraft valued at more than $14B from Boeing (BA), Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) and Embraer (ERJ) to update its current fleet and meet an anticipated increase in passenger demand.

ETFs to track Europe: ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

ETFs to track Asia & Oceania: (NYSEARCA:EWJ), (NYSE:JEQ), (NYSEARCA:FXI), (NASDAQ:PGJ), (NYSEARCA:EWH), (NYSEARCA:DXJ), (NYSE:CAF), (NYSEARCA:FXY), (NYSEARCA:YINN), (NYSEARCA:YANG), (NASDAQ:CXSE), (NYSEARCA:KWEB)

data.title: Earnings week ahead: TGT, COST, CRWD, AVGO, JD, PLUG, MRVL, and more
data.description:
As Wall Street steps into March, the earnings schedule offers a look at how a broad range of industries are navigating the current economic landscape.

Retailers take center stage, with industry leaders like Target (NYSE:TGT), Costco (NASDAQ:COST), Best Buy (NYSE:BBY) and Macy’s (NYSE:M) set to report their quarterly results. Meanwhile, Abercrombie & Fitch (NYSE:ANF), Ross Stores (NASDAQ:ROST) and The Gap (GAP) will add further insights into the retail sector.

Tech earnings will also be closely watched, with reports from Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO), CrowdStrike (NASDAQ:CRWD), JD.com (NASDAQ:JD), Hewlett Packard Enterprise (NYSE:HPE), MongoDB (NASDAQ:MDB) and Marvell Technology (NASDAQ:MRVL). These companies will offer key updates on enterprise demand, AI adoption and cybersecurity trends.

The renewable energy and EV infrastructure space will be represented as well, with reports from Plug Power (NASDAQ:PLUG), ChargePoint (NYSE:CHPT) and Stem (NYSE:STEM). In addition, earnings are also due out from AutoZone (NYSE:AZO), Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile (SQM) and Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ).

Below is a rundown of major quarterly updates anticipated in the week of March 3 to March 7:

Monday, March 3

GitLab (GTLB)

GitLab (GTLB) is set to report its Q4 results after the market closes on Monday. Analysts expect a 52% increase in profits on 26% Y/Y revenue growth. The stock has gained 7% so far this year, with both Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating system and Wall Street analysts assigning it a Strong Buy rating.

SA contributor HedgeMix highlights GitLab as a leading AI-powered DevSecOps platform with strong traction for GitLab Ultimate, now comprising ~50% of total ARR. HedgeMix rates GTLB as a Buy with a $75 price target, citing strong customer growth, high retention rates and favorable market trends.

  • Consensus EPS Estimates: $0.23
  • Consensus Revenue Estimates: $206.48M
  • Earnings Insight: GitLab has beaten EPS and revenue expectations in 8 straight quarters.

Also reporting: Okta (OKTA), Senseonics Holdings (SENS), Cara Therapeutics (CARA), TG Therapeutics (TGTX), Energous Corporation (WATT), Hut 8 (HUT), Ready Capital (RC), AST SpaceMobile (ASTS), Ocular Therapeutix (OCUL), ADMA Biologics (ADMA), Aqua Metals (AQMS), Emergent BioSolutions (EBS), Whitestone REIT (WSR) and more.

Tuesday, March 4

Target (TGT)

Target (TGT) is set to report its Q4 results on Tuesday morning, with mixed expectations following rival Walmart's report, which showed a strong Q4 performance but a weaker outlook. Analysts forecast a profit decline of over 28% alongside a slight revenue dip.

The retailer previously reported a 2.8% rise in holiday sales, driven by a 2% increase in comparable sales, record Black Friday and Cyber Monday sales, and a 9% surge in digital sales.

Morgan Stanley expects Target to adopt a cautious tone, citing weaker discretionary demand, rising SG&A expenses, and policy uncertainties around tariffs and immigration. Meanwhile, Wall Street analysts in general maintain a consensus Buy rating on the stock, which has dropped 9% YTD, while Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system holds a Neutral stance.

SA author Jason Ditz highlights Target’s strong holiday sales, lower P/E ratio and improved dividend yield as positives, though risks remain.

  • Consensus EPS Estimates: $2.25
  • Consensus Revenue Estimates: $30.85B
  • Earnings Insight: The company has beaten EPS expectations in 6 of the past 8 quarters, and revenue estimates in 5 of those reports.

Also reporting: Plug Power (PLUG), CrowdStrike Holdings (CRWD), Sea Limited (SE), ChargePoint Holdings (CHPT), Best Buy (BBY), Paysafe Limited (PSFE), Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile S.A. (SQM), bluebird bio (BLUE), Stem (STEM), Nordstrom (JWN), Ross Stores (ROST), Box (BOX), AutoZone (AZO), EVgo (EVGO), Celldex Therapeutics (CLDX) and more.

Wednesday, March 5

Zscaler (ZS)

Zscaler (ZS) is slated to report its FQ2 earnings results on Wednesday, after the market closes. Analysts expect a 9% Y/Y decline in profit despite a 21% revenue increase. While the stock has gained 6% YTD, it remains down 21% over the past year.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system double-upgraded the stock to a Strong Buy last month, while Wall Street analysts maintain a Buy rating.

Seeking Alpha contributor Real Investments takes a cautious stance, rating ZS as a Hold. The author argues that Zscaler's P/E ratio is excessively high, particularly when accounting for dilutive shares, making the stock overvalued despite revenue growth.

In contrast, The Alpha Sieve sees further upside, rating ZS a Buy. The author highlights Zscaler’s strong start to FY 2025, outperforming cybersecurity peers and the broader equity benchmark by 4x. Despite recent gains, the stock’s valuation remains reasonable, and technical indicators suggest bullish momentum.

  • Consensus EPS Estimates: $0.69
  • Consensus Revenue Estimates: $635.33M
  • Earnings Insight: Zscaler has consistently outperformed EPS and revenue expectations in the past 8 quarters.

Also reporting: Marvell Technology (MRVL), Editas Medicine (EDIT), Veeva Systems (VEEV), Stratasys (SSYS), MongoDB (MDB), Ocugen (OCGN), Dollar Tree (DLTR), Victoria's Secret & Co. (VSCO), Sangamo Therapeutics (SGMO), Foot Locker (FL), The Campbell's Company (CPB), TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC (TPVG) and more.

Thursday, March 6

Costco (COST)

Costco (COST) is scheduled to report its quarterly results after the market closes on Thursday, with analysts forecasting year-over-year growth in both revenue and earnings.

The company's December comparable sales (excluding gasoline and FX impacts) surged 9.9%, far exceeding the +5.2% consensus estimate. The gains were broad-based, with the U.S. up 9.8%, Canada up 10.3%, and international up 9.8%.

Additionally, e-commerce sales skyrocketed 35.7%, partly due to a calendar shift that placed Cyber Monday in December.

Total comparable sales rose 7.4%, nearly double the 3.7% consensus estimate. The momentum continued into January, with total comparable sales up 7.5%, again beating expectations (4.9% consensus).

Wall Street analysts remain optimistic, maintaining a Buy rating, while Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system has taken a more cautious stance with a Hold rating.

SA author Ken Taylor maintains a Hold rating, citing Costco’s high valuation despite its long-term growth strategy. The analyst praised Costco’s business model, which he sees thriving in both inflationary and recessionary periods, gaining market share due to its low-cost, high-value offerings. .

Meanwhile, SA contributor Skeptical12 holds a Buy rating, highlighting Costco’s distinct operational advantages compared to Walmart. The author emphasizes that while Walmart remains fundamentally strong, its management team is known for issuing conservative guidance, which may not fully reflect its underlying momentum.

  • Consensus EPS Estimates: $4.08
  • Consensus Revenue Estimates: $63.02B
  • Earnings Insight: Costco has exceeded EPS expectations in 7 of the past 8 quarters, missing revenue estimates three times in that span.

Also reporting: Broadcom (AVGO), JD.com (JD), Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (HPE), Macy's (M), The Kroger Co. (KR), Gevo (GEVO), Smith & Wesson Brands (SWBI), Mind Medicine (MNMD), Arcturus Therapeutics Holdings (ARCT), Cracker Barrel Old Country Store (CBRL), Xeris Biopharma Holdings (XERS), Fidus Investment (FDUS), Comstock (LODE), BJ's Wholesale Club Holdings (BJ), Funko (FNKO), Guidewire Software (GWRE), Domo (DOMO), Burlington Stores (BURL) and more.

Friday, March 7

Genesco (GCO)

Genesco (GCO) is set to report Q4 earnings before the market opens on Friday. In January, the company announced a 10% increase in comparable sales for Q4, with same-store sales up 6% and e-commerce sales rising 20%. It also reaffirmed its full-year EPS guidance of $0.80 to $1.00.

Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating system classifies the stock as a Strong Buy, while Wall Street analysts remain cautious with a Hold rating. SA author Quipus Capital noted that despite strong Q3 topline growth, concerns over operating leverage, margin improvement, and rising debt warrant a Hold stance on GCO.

  • Consensus EPS Estimates: $3.31
  • Consensus Revenue Estimates: $780.43M
  • Earnings Insight: The company has exceeded EPS expectations in 5 of the past 8 quarters, revenue estimates in 6 of those reports.

Also reporting: Algonquin Power & Utilities (AQN), Oncolytics Biotech (ONCY), YPF S.A. (YPF), WhiteHorse Finance (WHF), inTEST Corporation (INTT), Mammoth Energy Services (TUSK) and more.

data.title: Jeremy Grantham says U.S. stocks are in a 'super bubble'
data.description:
Legendary market strategist Jeremy Grantham said the U.S. stock market is in a "super bubble", ranking it the third behind Japan's bubble in 1989 and the real estate bubble of the same era.

"I've always looked at it from the point of view that the longer and the bigger and the higher it goes, the more exciting and dangerous it will be, and this has moved up the rank of super bubbles," said Grantham on a Bloomberg podcast interview.

"But it's nowhere near Japan, the mother and father of all super bubbles in ‘89, and it's nowhere near their real estate bubble of the same era," Grantham added.

This is not the first time the co-founder of asset management firm GMO has predicted doom. He warned investors to avoid U.S. stocks in 2024 and predicted a 50% drop in 2023. The S&P 500 (SP500) ended both years with gains exceeding 23%.

However, Grantham still commands a massive following due to him currently predicting the 2008 housing market crash and the dot-com bubble.

Calling tech, AI, and AI adjacent stocks overvalues, Grantham said he would feel safe investing in "greening the economy," without specifying particular assets.

While Grantham believes the future for "greening the economy" is "going to be a long and bumpy one," he called it necessary to prevent civilizational failure.

"Unlike most things in the stock market, I would say that is an area that we'll have sooner or later, a massive regrouping and a huge outperformance of the rest of the market," said Grantham.

More on S&P 500 Index

data.title: Google co-founder urges AI staff to 'turbocharge' efforts, work 60 hours per week
data.description:
Google co-founder Sergey Brin urged employees at the company's DeepMind AI division to "turbocharge" their efforts, as the company battles entrenched rivals and new entrants for supremacy in the generative AI race.

Brin, who stepped down as President of Google-parent Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) in 2019, urged Google's AI teams to work 60 hours a week, come into the office "at least every week day," prioritize simplicity, and move faster.

"It has been 2 years of the Gemini program and GDM. We have come a long way in that time with many efforts we should feel very proud of. At the same time competition has accelerated immensely and the final race to AGI is afoot. I think we have all the ingredients to win this race but we are going to have to turbocharge our efforts," wrote Brin in a memo shared by The Verge.

Google's current policy requires employees to work in the office at least three days a week.

Notably, the memo was sent by Brin, who currently does not hold a formal role inside Google besides being a board member, and not DeepMind chief executive Demis Hassabis.

Brin also asked employees to stop "building nanny products" and "trust our users" more, saying that Google's AI products are "overrun with filters and punts of various kinds."

Microsoft (MSFT)- backed OpenAI triggered an AI arms race with launch of ChatGPT in November 2022, catching Google off-guard. Since then, Silicon Valley has poured hundreds of billions of dollars into developing their own AI models, with Google launching its Gemini family of large language models.

Last December, Google CEO Sundar Pichai warned employees that 2025 would be a challenging year, as the search giant faces "scrutiny across the world."

More on Alphabet

data.title: IRA tax credit repeal could drive up electricity prices, lead to power shortfalls, reports say
data.description:
U.S. consumers may pay more for electricity over the next decade if Congress repeals the Inflation Reduction Act's Section 45Y investment and Section 48E production tax credits for clean electricity, UtilityDive.com reported this week, citing new studies from The Brattle Group and NERA Economic Consulting.

Full repeal of the 45Y and 48E tax credits would raise delivered electricity prices nationwide by 7.3% for residential customers and 10.6% for commercial and industrial customers, or 9.2% across all sectors, by 2029, according to the NERA report that was written for the Clean Energy Buyers Association.

"The lack of technology-neutral tax incentives has the effect of increasing the electricity prices in both cost-of-service and competitive regions as electricity demand must be met by relatively more expensive generating technologies," the NERA/CEBA study said.

Full repeal could reduce direct spending in the power sector by $250B and result in a $510B decline in U.S. gross domestic product through 2035 compared with current projections, Brattle said in its report for ConservAmerica.

Electricity demand is expected to increase by 50% over the next decade due to rising demand from data centers, re-shored manufacturing, electrification of industry and increased oil and gas production, and solar, wind and batteries are the resources best equipped to meet demand in the near term, according to the Brattle/ConservAmerica report.

Electricity prices are tracking toward the highest since the 1990s when factoring in inflation, as demand skyrockets, according to a new report from the American Enterprise Institute.

Meanwhile, electricity supply from coal, which was once the primary source of U.S. electricity, has dropped to 16%, and U.S. power generators plan to remove ~8.1 GW of coal-fired power generation capacity this year, which would roughly double the amount that was retired in 2024, the Energy Information Administration said this week.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:XLE), (NYSEARCA:XLU), (ICLN), (QCLN), (PBW), (PBD), (ACES), (CNRG), (ERTH), (SMOG)

data.title: Boeing to halt deliveries of Pegasus refueling tankers after discovering cracks: report
data.description:
The U.S. Air Force will inspect its entire fleet of 89 Boeing (NYSE:BA) KC-46A Pegasus aerial refueling tankers after cracks were found on two of the four new planes set to be sent to the Military Delivery Center, The War Zone reported Saturday.

The issue was discovered by Boeing (BA), the report said.

Boeing (BA) "is taking measures to temporarily halt deliveries until we fully understand the root cause and implement an effective corrective action plan," the Air Force told The War Zone.

"We are working closely with the customer to assess a potential issue on KC-46 aircraft and to mitigate any potential impact to the fleet and in-production airplanes," said Boeing in a statement.

The cracks were not found on any flight surfaces or hinges, but rather on the primary and secondary structures, the report said.

In November, the planemaker secured a $2.4 billion contract from from the Air Force to supply 15 additional KC-46A Pegasus aerial refueling tankers.

The Pegasus program has faced issues for years, with Boeing (BA) delivering the first plane in 2019, after years of delay.

data.title: Guyana says Venezuelan naval ship approached Exxon-contracted ship in Straboek field
data.description:
Guyana’s government said Saturday that a Venezuelan naval patrol ship entered its waters and approached a vessel contracted by Exxon Mobil (NYSE:XOM) in the Straboek block, according to media reports.

Guyana President Irfaan Ali said the armed vessel informed the vessel via a radio message that it was operating in disputed waters before it continued on to other ships in the area, Bloomberg reported.

“During this incursion, the Venezuelan vessel approached various assets in our exclusive waters, including FPSO Prosperity,” Ali reportedly told Bloomberg, referring to Exxon-contracted ship.

Ali was quoted as saying that the Guyana Defense Force ordered aircraft to the area and a Coast Guard ship was en route.

He said Venezuela’s ambassador to Guyana has been summoned for a meeting and his government would dispatch a formal note of protest to Caracas, Bloomberg reported.

The Organization of American States condemned Venezuela’s actions. “Such acts of intimidation constitute a clear violation of international law, undermine regional stability, and threaten the principles of peaceful coexistence between nations,” the OAS said in a statement.

Neither Exxon Mobil nor the Venezuelan mission to the United Nations in New York immediately responded to requests for comment by Seeking Alpha. The White House could not be reached for comment.

data.title: SEC nixes wave of crypto probes and suits in Trump-era reset
data.description:
The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission this week scrapped a slew of lawsuits and investigations targeting crypto-linked companies, marking a notable shift in its approach to crypto regulation amid the Trump administration's pro-crypto push.

Shortly after taking office last month, President Donald Trump tasked crypto-friendly Paul Atkins, who is awaiting confirmation as the new SEC chair, to develop a comprehensive, perhaps more favorable, regulatory framework for crypto assets. The regulator is currently led by Acting Chair Mark Uyeda.

As such, the SEC formed last month its own crypto task force, dismissing a growing number of suits brought against crypto-tied firms under the Biden administration.

On the campaign trail, Trump pledged to make the U.S. a global hub for the crypto space.

Notably, Coinbase Global's (COIN) legal battle with the SEC came to an end on Thursday, as the SEC tossed its litigation against the U.S.'s largest crypto exchange. In 2023, the SEC filed the suit, alleging that the company was operating as an unregistered securities broker. Robinhood Markets (HOOD), a retail-focused stock and crypto trading platform, disclosed on Feb. 24 that the SEC closed its probe into its crypto business without taking any enforcement action.

On Feb. 13, the SEC's suit against crypto giant Binance was put on hold by a federal judge in Washington, D.C., though it's not fully dismissed.

Cases targeting the following companies were also axed: OpenSea, the most well-known non-fungible token (NFT) marketplace, decentralized crypto exchange Uniswap, and blockchain software firm Consensys. Some cases remain active or unresolved, though. Crypto exchange Kraken, for instance, is still facing a suit from two years ago over unregistered securities.

On top of the recent wave of dismissals, the SEC issued long-sought guidance that effectively leaves meme coins outside its regulatory purview, declaring them more like collectibles than securities. Before Trump took office last month, then-SEC Chair Gary Gensler proposed that meme coins and other crypto tokens should be deemed as securities, not commodities. Note that Gensler did oversee the approval and subsequent launch of the first-ever U.S.-listed spot bitcoin (BTC-USD) and ether (ETH-USD) exchange-traded funds last year.

The SEC's shift from its once-hostile crypto stance aims to give firms the space to innovate and create projects, while shielding investors from fraud, Commissioner Hester Peirce, who heads the agency's crypto task force, had said in a Feb. 4 statement.

Last month, the agency repealed the controversial SAB 121 rule, which required banks and other publicly-traded companies to account for customers' crypto assets on their balance sheets, deterring them from custody services.

Meanwhile, jitters around tariffs and inflation have dragged crypto prices down markedly. The price of bitcoin (BTC-USD) retreated around 12% over the past five days as of Friday afternoon, its steepest decline since the FTX collapse in late 2022. Ether (ETH-USD) dropped ~19% over the same period.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: UnitedHealth leads Humana, CVS as Medicare Advantage growth cools
data.description:
Americans enrolled in Medicare Advantage plans rose just over 3% from 2024 to 2025, marking one of the lowest growth rates for the program in years, healthcare policy organization KFF said this week, citing the latest federal enrollment data.

Among MA operators, UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH) has further established its market leadership, while Humana (NYSE:HUM), the second-largest MA player, and CVS Health (NYSE:CVS) have both shed memberships, according to a report from Healthcare Dive.

Medicare Advantage (MA) plans had enrolled slightly over 34.4M Americans as of Feb. 1, indicating a 3.8% rise from a year ago and marking the program’s lowest growth rate in more than a decade, KFF said.

During the recently concluded open enrollment period, UnitedHealthcare, the insurance unit of UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH), had expanded its MA memberships to 9.9M from 9.5M in December, while its rivals Humana (NYSE:HUM) and CVS Health’s (NYSE:CVS) Aetna division have both lost members.

HUM (NYSE:HUM), whose benefits ratio climbed to ~90% from ~87% amid a post-COVID spike in medical care among its enrollees, witnessed its membership base falling from 6.2M to 5.8M, roughly in line with the company’s target to shed approximately 550K MA members this year.

While MA memberships at CVS’ (NYSE:CVS) and Molina Healthcare (MOH) have dropped to ~4.2M and ~216.7K from ~4.4M and 244.7K, respectively, Centene’s (CNC) MA memberships held relatively steady at ~1.1M.

However, Elevance Health (ELV) and Cigna (CI) have expanded their MA memberships to ~2.3M and ~697.4K from ~2.0M and ~587.9K, respectively.
